front: From which three embryonic tissue sources does the eye develop?
back: Neuroectoderm (retina, optic nerve, iris/ciliary epithelium, sphincter and dilator muscles), surface ectoderm (lens, corneal epithelium, conjunctival epithelium, lacrimal gland), and neural crest-derived mesenchyme (corneal stroma and endothelium, sclera, choroidal stroma, trabecular meshwork, ciliary muscle).

front: When do the optic grooves (optic sulci) first appear in human embryonic development?
back: Around day 22 of gestation, as bilateral evaginations of the forebrain neuroectoderm. They deepen into optic vesicles by around day 25.

front: What structure induces lens placode formation, and from which germ layer does the lens placode arise?
back: The optic vesicle (neuroectoderm) induces the overlying surface ectoderm to thicken into the lens placode around week 4. The lens is entirely surface ectoderm-derived.

front: How does the optic cup form, and which layer becomes the neural retina vs. the RPE?
back: The optic vesicle invaginates to form a double-walled optic cup. The inner layer becomes the neural retina; the outer layer becomes the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE).

front: What is the embryonic (choroidal) fissure, and what passes through it?
back: A groove on the inferonasal surface of the optic cup and stalk that allows the hyaloid artery to enter the developing eye. It normally closes by week 7.

front: Failure of the embryonic fissure to close produces what congenital anomaly?
back: Coloboma — a defect classically located inferonasally affecting iris, ciliary body, choroid, retina, and/or optic nerve depending on the extent of closure failure.

front: From what does the lens vesicle form, and what happens to its posterior cells?
back: The lens placode invaginates and pinches off from surface ectoderm to form the lens vesicle. Posterior cells elongate anteriorly to form the primary lens fibers, obliterating the vesicle lumen.

front: What is the embryologic origin of the corneal epithelium, stroma, and endothelium?
back: Epithelium derives from surface ectoderm. Stroma and endothelium derive from neural crest mesenchyme that migrates in waves between the lens vesicle and surface ectoderm.

front: Which intraocular muscles are neuroectodermal in origin, unlike most muscles in the body?
back: The iris sphincter and dilator muscles — both derive from the anterior neuroectoderm of the optic cup, not from mesoderm. The ciliary muscle, by contrast, is neural crest-derived.

front: What is the hyaloid artery, and what is its fate?
back: A branch of the ophthalmic artery that nourishes the developing lens and primary vitreous via the embryonic fissure. It regresses by the third trimester; persistence causes persistent fetal vasculature (PFV).

front: Distinguish primary, secondary, and tertiary vitreous.
back: Primary vitreous: vascularized, contains hyaloid system; regresses. Secondary vitreous: avascular, becomes the definitive adult vitreous. Tertiary vitreous: forms the zonular fibers of the lens.

front: What is Bergmeister's papilla, and what does it represent?
back: A small glial remnant of the hyaloid artery at the optic disc. It is a benign developmental remnant of incomplete hyaloid regression.

front: What is a Mittendorf dot?
back: A small white opacity on the posterior lens capsule, typically inferonasal, representing a remnant of the attachment of the hyaloid artery to the lens. Benign and visually insignificant.

front: From which embryonic structure do the extraocular muscles develop?
back: Paraxial mesoderm (specifically preotic somitomeres). This is a notable exception — most other periocular structures derive from neural crest, not mesoderm.

front: What is the embryologic origin of the sclera and choroid?
back: Both derive primarily from neural crest mesenchyme that condenses around the optic cup. The posterior sclera has some mesodermal contribution.

front: When during gestation does retinal vascularization begin and complete?
back: Retinal vessels begin growing from the optic disc around week 16 and reach the nasal ora serrata by ~36 weeks and the temporal ora serrata by ~40 weeks. Incomplete vascularization in preterm infants underlies retinopathy of prematurity.

front: What is the embryologic origin of the optic nerve fibers and its myelination pattern?
back: Optic nerve axons are extensions of retinal ganglion cells (neuroectoderm). Myelination proceeds from the chiasm distally toward the eye and normally stops at the lamina cribrosa shortly after birth.

front: How do the eyelids develop, and when do they separate?
back: Eyelid folds of surface ectoderm and neural crest mesenchyme fuse around week 8–9 and remain fused until approximately week 25–26 of gestation, when they separate.

front: Maldevelopment of neural crest-derived anterior segment structures produces what spectrum of disorders?
back: Anterior segment dysgenesis — including Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome, Peters anomaly, and posterior embryotoxon. These reflect abnormal neural crest migration or differentiation, often with associated glaucoma risk.

front: Approximately when in gestation is the basic structural development of the eye complete?
back: Major structural development is largely complete by the end of the embryonic period (~week 8), though functional maturation — particularly of the macula, myelination, and refractive components — continues well after birth.
